name: chamomile
mechanism: likely involves multi-component phytochemistry (for example apigenin-related gabaergic and anti-inflammatory hypotheses) | but definitive clinical mechanism remains unresolved
contraindications: avoid use in individuals with known severe ragweed/asteraceae allergy
interactions: Caution with: Potential additive sedation with sedative drugs
Caution with: monitor anticoagulant co use cautiously in high intake extract contexts
safetyNotes: Generally well tolerated, but allergic reactions can occur, especially in Asteraceae-sensitive individuals.
description: Chamomile is frequently used as a tea-first herb for gentle evening calming routines and bedtime wind-down habits.
dosage: Tea and extract labels vary widely; compare by flower amount per serving and keep preparations consistent before adjusting.
duration: Often used situationally or nightly in short-to-medium routines depending on tolerance and sleep goals.
region: Native to Europe and Western Asia
therapeuticUses: Mild stress-tension support in non-daytime-critical settings
traditionalUse: Traditional use includes digestive and calming applications across European and Middle Eastern herbal traditions.
categoryUseContext: Best known for tea-based calming use, light digestive comfort pairing, and low-friction nightly routines.
evidenceLevel: Moderate traditional and limited modern clinical support for calming use contexts; strongest confidence is around practical tolerability and routine fit.
